review_text: The smell of butter is very strong you look great but the taste is very mediocre compared to the price
issue: The butter smell is overly strong and unpleasant
fixes: Install a calibrated odor sensor (e.g., Sensirion SGP 40) in the butter‑storage area and set it to trigger an automatic alert in the facility’s MES when VOC levels exceed 150 ppb, prompting immediate ventilation or product removal. | - Deploy a digital HACCP checklist in iAuditor that requires staff to log temperature and time‑in‑storage for each batch; configure the app to flag any batch exceeding 48 hours at ≥15 °C before release. | - Introduce a weekly sensory panel using a standardized ISO 8586 protocol, recording aroma intensity scores in a Google Sheet dashboard and mandating corrective action if the average score exceeds 8/10. | - Replace the current packaging with a barrier film containing a built‑in antioxidant scavenger (e.g., BHT‑impregnated polyethylene) and validate its effectiveness through a 30‑day A/B test, measuring residual odor on finished goods with a handheld gas chromatography unit (e.g., PerkinElmer GC).

review_text: The smell of butter is very strong you look great but the taste is very mediocre compared to the price
issue: The taste is mediocre and does not meet expectations
fixes: Deploy a standardized sensory evaluation panel (e.g., ISO 8586) that scores each new recipe on flavor intensity, sweetness, and umami; require a minimum average score of 8/10 before product launch. | - Integrate ChefTec recipe‑management software to lock down exact ingredient weights, cooking times, and temperatures, then generate daily variance reports and adjust any batch deviating >5% from the master formula. | - Conduct a weekly blind taste test with 20 target consumers using SurveyMonkey NPS surveys; set a target NPS ≥70 and trigger a corrective‑action ticket in Jira if the threshold isn’t met. | - Implement IoT temperature‑controlled storage units (e.g., Monnit sensors) linked to a dashboard that alerts chefs when storage conditions drift beyond ±2 °C of optimal range, ensuring consistent flavor profiles across production runs.

issue: The product is overpriced relative to its quality
fixes: Deploy a price‑benchmarking dashboard in Power BI that pulls competitor pricing from scraping tools (e.g., Import.io) and automatically flags any SKU whose margin falls below the target threshold, triggering a review workflow in Jira. | - Introduce a tiered “value bundle” structure using SAP CPQ to group high‑margin accessories with core products, reducing perceived price by up to 15 % while preserving overall profit. | - Implement a quarterly quality‑to‑price audit in Minitab that measures defect rates per unit; if defects exceed a predefined tolerance, adjust the cost‑of‑goods‑sold in Oracle NetSuite to reflect corrective actions before the next price revision. | - Launch a post‑purchase NPS survey via Qualtrics that includes a “price‑fairness” question, and set a KPI to increase the “price meets expectations” rating by at least 10 points within six months, driving iterative pricing adjustments.

review_text: The strawberry cheesecake was 7/10 (good but not the best). The brownie was 5/10 (just average American pastry with too much sugar and not that much flavor). Coffees were disappointing, below average. The place was disappointing as the place right in front was amazing.
issue: Strawberry cheesecake was only average, not outstanding
fixes: Deploy a standardized sensory evaluation protocol (e.g., ISO 8589) with a trained tasting panel that scores each batch on texture, flavor intensity, and sweetness; require a minimum average score of 8/10 before release. | - Integrate ChefTec recipe management software to lock in exact ingredient weights, mixing times, and bake temperatures, then run weekly variance reports and adjust formulations until the target moisture content (≥ 55 %) is met. | - Install IoT‑enabled temperature data loggers (such as ThermoWorks ChefSensor) on ovens and freezers, set alerts for any deviation beyond ±2 °C, and record compliance rates in a daily KPI dashboard. | - Conduct a supplier quality audit using the SQF‑level 2 checklist, negotiate a “freshness‑guarantee” clause with the strawberry vendor, and track delivery age in the ERP system to ensure ≤ 24 h post‑harvest. | - Launch a limited‑time R&D pilot where the pastry team creates three prototype cheesecake variations, tests them against the current baseline using a cost‑benefit model in Excel, and selects the top performer based on a weighted scoring matrix.

review_text: The strawberry cheesecake was 7/10 (good but not the best). The brownie was 5/10 (just average American pastry with too much sugar and not that much flavor). Coffees were disappointing, below average. The place was disappointing as the place right in front was amazing.
issue: Brownie was overly sweet and lacked flavor
fixes: Standardize the recipe with precise measurements:** Use a digital kitchen scale (e.g., Ohaus Scout) and record each ingredient weight in grams into a shared Google Sheet formula that automatically calculates sweetness percentage based on sugar‑to‑flour ratio, then flag any batch exceeding 18 % sweetness for review before baking. | - **Add a calibrated sensory panel:** Conduct weekly blind taste tests with three trained tasters using a 9‑point hedonic scale; log scores in a Trello board that triggers an automatic Slack alert if average sweetness rating exceeds 7.0. | - **Integrate real‑time oven temperature monitoring:** Install a ThermoWorks Thermapen ONE probe linked to a cloud dashboard (e.g., Zapier) that records bake time/temperature data; set alerts when actual internal brownie temperature deviates more than ±2 °C from the target 175 °F, indicating uneven cooking that may affect flavor development. | - **Control natural flavor intensity via ingredient quality scores:** Source cocoa powder and vanilla extract from vendors rated ≥ 90 % on the Supplier Quality Scorecard in SAP Ariba, and require quarterly supplier audits; automatically reject lots that fall below the threshold to ensure consistent flavor profile. | - **Deploy a QR‑code feedback loop:** Place a QR code on each brownie box that links to a one‑question SurveyMonkey form asking “Rate sweetness (1‑10)”; aggregate responses daily in Power BI and trigger a corrective action ticket in Jira whenever the average score exceeds 8.0.

review_text: I went here whilst staying at The Venetian hotel over New Year's Eve and honestly had a terrible experience. I was very excited to visit the bakery as I used to watch cake boss on TV and absolutely LOVED the show. I tried the lobster tail which is highly overrated, the cupcakes which were dry and a few other pastries that aren't even worth mentioning as I don't even remember anymore they left no impression on me. Definitely does not deserve to have its own tv show- there are much better bakeries out there.
issue: Lobster tail was disappointing and not worth the hype
fixes: Deploy a standardized sensory evaluation sheet (e.g., ISO 8589‑based) for every lobster batch, requiring chefs to record texture, flavor intensity, and overall satisfaction scores before service; any batch scoring below 8/10 triggers immediate recipe adjustment. | - Install IoT temperature sensors on all seafood storage units (e.g., SmarterChef Smart Temp) and integrate alerts into the kitchen’s Slack channel so staff are notified in real time if temperatures deviate from the optimal 0 °C range, preventing over‑cooking. | - Implement a weekly supplier quality audit checklist in the restaurant’s ERP (e.g., Toast POS) that records freshness date, origin certification, and visual inspection results; suppliers falling short of the predefined criteria receive a corrective‑action ticket in Asana within 24 hours. | - Create a “Hype vs. Reality” menu card prototype with QR‑linked Google Forms feedback, asking diners to rate the lobster against expectations; analyze responses daily and adjust portion size, garnish, or pricing accordingly when average ratings fall below 4 stars.

review_text: Well I didn't go but I am sure the food is good. I was not going to wait over an hour in line to be rushed through. If I had known I would not have wasted my time going. Very disappointed.
issue: Long wait time of over an hour in line
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based virtual queue (e.g., QLess) that issues customers SMS tickets and displays real‑time wait estimates on digital signage, reducing perceived wait time by at least 20 %. | - Integrate NICE Workforce Management with the POS system to forecast peak traffic from historical sales data and schedule additional cashiers 15 minutes before predicted spikes, cutting average queue length by up to 30 %. | - Install self‑service kiosks (using Square Terminal or Toast) for routine transactions such as check‑in and order placement, targeting a 25 % reduction in staff‑driven line activity within six weeks. | - Implement a dynamic staffing model using Tableau dashboards linked to real‑time queue metrics; trigger automatic alerts when the queue exceeds five minutes so supervisors can dispatch float employees instantly.

fixes: Deploy a real‑time queue dashboard in Zendesk Explore that triggers an automatic “service pause” alert when an agent’s pending ticket count exceeds their pre‑defined capacity (e.g., 8 open tickets), forcing them to close one before taking new requests. | - Institute a mandatory 2‑minute “wrap‑up buffer” at the end of every interaction logged in Salesforce Service Cloud; supervisors review compliance weekly via a built‑in report on average buffer usage. | - Integrate a speech‑analytics module from CallMiner into the call center, flagging any utterances containing phrases like “hurry up” or “quickly,” and schedule quarterly coaching sessions focused on reducing those tags by 15 % per quarter. | - Launch a self‑service knowledge hub powered by Intercom’s Custom Articles feature, embedding a “quick‑answer” widget on key product pages that agents must click through before picking up a live chat, thereby cutting direct handoffs by an estimated 10 % within three months.

review_text: I was so excited to go here, but that excitement soon turned to disappointment. We had never been to a Carlo's Bakery in person, and we are fans of Buddy, of course, so we had to go while in Vegas. We waited in a decently long line, which gave us time to look at all the things that were wrong with this place. The display cases were filthy and messy. Frosting, sprinkles and crumbs were everywhere. There were even some cupcakes that had fallen upside down that just laid there in the case looking oh so sad. Several of the trays were empty, one of those being cannolis. One of the main reasons we wanted to go to Carlo's was was to try one of their famous cannolis. Sadly, that didn't happen. Mind you this was in the middle of the day. It wasn't like we were there 10 minutes til close. On top of that the food that was in the case was not properly labeled. Many things had no labels at all. This can be dangerous for folks with food allergies and annoying to other people trying to communicate what they want to the workers. We ended up getting a lobster tail and a napoleon type thing (a layered cake. no sign, so who knows what it was!). The lobster tails tasted stale and like it had sat in a fridge for a week. The cake thing was just not good. The flavor was not good. I took one bite of each and wished I had spent that $20 on a slot machine instead of desserts that wal-mart can do better than. I really hope Buddy and his family get a handle on things and get their locations back in shape, because I don't think this is the quality they had in mind for their customers.
issue: Display cases were filthy and messy with frosting, sprinkles, crumbs, and fallen cupcakes
fixes: Deploy a daily “Case‑Clean” checklist in iAuditor (SafetyCulture) that assigns a staff member to wipe down each display case with approved cleaning solution, log completion time, and trigger automatic escalation if any item is missed twice in a row. | - Install QR‑code stickers on each case linking to a one‑click Workforce Management task in Deputy; when scanned, the system creates a 15‑minute window for cleaning, automatically notifying the assigned shift lead via Slack. | - Schedule a weekly deep‑clean rotation using a standardized SOP documented in Confluence, with a KPI of ≤ 2 minutes per case and a real‑time compliance dashboard in Power BI that flags any deviation from the target. | - Integrate RFID sensors (e.g., Zebra MotionWorks) into case lids to detect when they are opened, generating an instant alert to the floor supervisor’s mobile device so cleaning can be performed before the next product placement.

review_text: Very disappointing, got the fabled Cannoli's and what an absolute waste of money. I had actually gotten some from a different bakery at Paris and assumed that as well as those tasted, Carlos's would be better, boy was I wrong! Buddy needs to stick to making cakes that look good, and I'm sure don't taste good.

issue: The product was considered a waste of money, indicating poor value for money
fixes: Deploy a post‑purchase NPS survey in Qualtrics that triggers automatically after 48 hours and includes a “value rating” question; use the resulting score (≤6) to create a targeted “value recovery” email workflow in HubSpot that offers a complimentary upgrade or refund within 7 days. | - Integrate a ROI calculator into the product’s onboarding flow using Typeform, allowing customers to input usage metrics and receive real‑time cost‑benefit estimates before they commit, reducing perceived waste by showing tangible value. | - Implement a tiered warranty program managed through ServiceNow that automatically issues replacement units or service credits when a defect is logged, turning negative perceptions into positive experiences and demonstrating measurable risk mitigation. | - Launch a quarterly “Value Impact” webinar series hosted on Zoom, where subject‑matter experts present case studies and run interactive polls to quantify return‑on‑investment; track attendance and conversion rates via Google Analytics to gauge effectiveness.

review_text: Awful experience. Guest specialist Alyssa had a horrible attitude. She should be fired. The cashier Akberet, however, was very polite. Thumbs down for bad service!
issue: Guest specialist displayed a horrible attitude, leading to a bad service experience
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment monitoring dashboard (e.g., Medallia Experience Cloud) that flags any “negative” interaction score within 15 minutes; automatically route those cases to a supervisor for immediate coaching. | - Require all guest specialists to complete a quarterly 360° feedback cycle in SurveyMonkey, with a minimum average rating of 4.5/5 on professionalism; tie results to performance bonuses and trigger corrective action if scores fall below target. | - Implement weekly 20‑minute role‑play drills on the Lessonly LMS where staff rehearse handling difficult customers, recording each session and reviewing it against a standardized scoring rubric. | - Create a “Zero‑Tolerance Escalation SOP” that mandates a documented escalation workflow in Zendesk: any guest complaint about rude behavior auto‑assigns to a senior manager and triggers a mandatory follow‑up call within 24 hours. | - Launch a peer‑shadowing program through the Workforce Management module in Kronos, pairing each specialist with a top‑rated colleague for one day per month to observe best‑practice interactions and capture learnings in a shared Google Sheet.

review_text: Place was horrible. First and foremost, we grabbed a number which was B89 and waited over 2 hours for our number to get called. One of the most disorganized companies I have ever seen. I understand things were busy but the way of doing business was horrible and we noticed a lot of people leaving purely because of the wait. Second, once we finally got our number called, I was informed the pie that I had ordered 4 hours earlier was not made. The manager came out and spoke to me and before even apologizing, blamed it on me because I had not pre-paid. She tried to explain how the process worked if I would have pre-paid for the pie but did not apologize or offer a solution outside of waiting another 40 minutes for my pie to be made. Absolute worst customer service I have ever seen from a "manager." On top of that, pre-paying for my pie was never even provided to me as an option which only made things worse. Would not recommend this to anyone unless you are looking to waste a few hours on the strip and if you don't want to get your order correct.
issue: Excessive wait time of over two hours before being called
fixes: Deploy a real‑time queue dashboard in Zendesk Explore that triggers an automatic alert to supervisors when average wait time exceeds 45 minutes, prompting immediate staffing adjustments. | - Integrate Twilio Flex with the call center’s CRM to route callers to the next available agent within 90 seconds and display live SLA compliance metrics on a wallboard. | - Implement predictive staffing using NICE Workforce Management to forecast peak call volume 15 minutes ahead and schedule additional agents during identified “high‑wait” windows. | - Launch an AI‑driven IVR chatbot (e.g., Ada) that resolves common inquiries and offers callback options, reducing inbound calls by at least 20 % and cutting average hold time.

issue: No pre‑payment option provided, leading to confusion about ordering process
fixes: Deploy Stripe Checkout’s “Pre‑order” button on the product page (or Shopify Buy Now) and configure it so the customer sees “Pay Later” before confirming the order, ensuring they know upfront whether payment is immediate or scheduled. | - Add a static FAQ banner to every checkout flow that includes a one‑click link to the payment terms page, hosted on your existing Help Center, and track its click‑through rate via Google Analytics events. | - Create a dedicated “Pre‑Order” product type in your eCommerce platform (e.g., WooCommerce) with a custom label and required “Pre‑pay date” field, then run weekly automated UAT tests using the “Pre‑Order Test User” script to verify that the field populates correctly. | - Integrate a real‑time payment status widget powered by a Zapier workflow that pulls data from your payment gateway (e.g., PayPal) into the order confirmation email, displaying “Pending Pre‑Payment – Payment due by [date]” and measuring success through email open rates and complaint tickets.

review_text: Don't send your child in alone. The girl behind the counter told our grandson the machine was broken so his kid cup was filled only one-third of the way (about 5 tablespoons) and watery, although he still paid full price and tipped. When he returned to the car he was obviously disappointed so I went in to ask about it. The shift manager told me that's how they come now and then added, "Have a nice day." I guess rudeness and minion size portions is how they roll! As for us, we walked over to Culliver's where my grandson got a real size cup of ice cream with great customer service!
issue: Customer received a significantly smaller, watery portion of ice cream but was charged full price.
fixes: Deploy calibrated digital scales (e.g., Ohaus Scout) at the point‑of‑sale so every ice‑cream scoop is automatically weighed; set a threshold alert in the POS system when the weight falls below 70 % of the standard portion size. | - Install RFID‑enabled portion cups linked to the inventory management software (e.g., Oracle NetSuite) that logs exact cup fill volume per transaction and triggers a real‑time “underfill” flag if the volume is <90 %. | - Add a QR‑code receipt addendum generated by the POS that shows the actual gram count versus the advertised serving, enabling customers to verify immediately and submit a claim through the brand’s mobile app. | - Implement a weekly audit checklist in iAuditor where store managers record scoop weights and compare them against the target metric; any deviation >10 % generates an automatic ticket in ServiceNow for corrective action. | - Launch a rapid‑response compensation workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud that auto‑creates a coupon code for the next purchase within 24 hours whenever an underfilled order is flagged by the monitoring systems.

issue: Staff, including the manager, responded rudely and dismissively to the complaint.
fixes: Deploy a mandatory “Live Call Debrief” workflow in the CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) where every interaction flagged by the quality scorecard ≥ 70 automatically creates a 15‑minute coaching ticket assigned to the team lead within 24 hours. | - Institute a weekly 20‑minute role‑play drill using the video‑analysis feature in the call‑center platform (e.g., NICE inContact), focusing on de‑escalation scripts and requiring a post‑session scoring report with ≥ 85 % compliance before the next shift. | - Integrate a real‑time sentiment dashboard (e.g., Medallia for CX) that triggers an instant Slack alert to the floor supervisor when a “negative tone” flag is recorded, prompting immediate follow‑up and corrective action within 30 minutes. | - Launch a quarterly mystery‑shopper program through a vetted vendor, delivering detailed rubrics on professionalism; tie results to individual performance bonuses and publish aggregate scores on the company intranet to reinforce accountability.
